coredata.dc:title: Bamboo stem biomass waste-derived excitation-dependent carbon dots for nanomolar detection of fungicide dodine in real samples and their pH-sensitive bacterial interaction studies
coredata.dc:creator: Adaikalapandi, Subitha
Thangadurai, T. Daniel
Manjubaashini, N.
Nataraj, D.
Babu, T.G. Satheesh
Kumar, S. Megha
coredata.dc:description: The present work investigates the mitigation of dodine (DD) fungicide residue, which is recognized for its adverse environmental consequences, by the use of carbon dots (CDs) prepared from bamboo stem biomass (BS-CDs). This work focuses on the synthesis of BS-CDs using the one-step hydrothermal method, which has been shown the remarkable sensing efficiency even at low concentrations. The synthesized BS-CDs were analyzed through X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic techniques (HRTEM). The BS-CDs were spherical and mono-dispersed, with an average size of 3.71 nm. By utilizing the fluorescence quenching property of BS-CDs, a detection limit of 4.3 nM for DD was successfully achieved. The non-toxic profile of BS-CDs has been confirmed by cytotoxicity studies using MTT assay. Application research mostly focuses on exploring the interaction between CDs and bacteria, hence elucidating their pH-sensitive characteristics. Real sample (fruits and vegetables) analysis was carried out to assess the viability of the detection approach, and the results showed respectable recovery rates (105 to 115 %) with a relative standard deviation (∼2 %). The findings provide a positive outlook for addressing the presence of DD residue in ecologically vulnerable regions, emphasizing the viability of using BS-CDs as an environmentally friendly remedy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bamboo stem
Carbon dots
Dodine
Bacteria
pH-sensitive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bacterial stain cultivation and BS-CD labeling
Selectivity studies of BS-CDs with dodine
Determination of DD in real sample analysis
Fluorescence analysis of the interaction of BS-CDs and DD with S. aureus

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Author SA and TDT sincerely acknowledge the KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology for funding through an Institutional research fellowship, a Seed grant to conduct research, and for providing XRD and UV–Vis spectroscopy characterization facilities. NM admits her gratitude to the Department of Biotechnology, New Delhi for providing financial support under the DBT-Research Associateship program. DN would like to express his gratitude to the UGC-CPEPA program on Advanced Studies in Physics for the development of Solar Energy Materials and Devices of Bharathiar University for providing the facilities. Authors SA and TDT thank SAIF-MGU, Kottayam, for the Raman characterization studies.

Author SA and TDT sincerely acknowledge the KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology for funding through an Institutional research fellowship, a Seed grant to conduct research, and for providing XRD and UV–Vis spectroscopy characterization facilities. NM admits her gratitude to the Department of Biotechnology, New Delhi for providing financial support under the DBT-Research Associateship program. DN would like to express his gratitude to the UGC-CPEPA program on Advanced Studies in Physics for the development of Solar Energy Materials and Devices of Bharathiar University for providing the facilities. Authors SA and TDT thank SAIF-MGU, Kottayam, for the Raman characterization studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of BS-CDs with a scale bar of a) 20 nm, b) 30 nm, c) and d) 100 nm.
a) HRTEM images of BS-CDs synthesized from bamboo stem, b) size distribution histogram, c) lattice fringes of single BS-CDs, and d) SAED pattern.
XPS of BS-CDs. a) Survey spectrum, b) C1s, c) O1s, and d) N1s.
a) Raman, and b) FTIR spectrum of BS-CDs.
(a) UV absorption, and (b) photoluminescence (PL) emission at varied excitation wavelengths (310 → 380 nm) of BS-CDs. (inset (a) BS-CDs solution under UV-light and visible light).

Selectivity study for CDs towards DD: a) fluorescence emission spectra of BS-CDs with different fungicides (100 μM) (Myclomain (My), Zineb (Z), dodine DD), Mancozeb (Ma), Carbendazim (C)) (λex = 380 nm), and b) corresponding bar diagram with standard deviation.
(a) Fluorescence quenching of BS-CDs with varied concentrations of DD (0.1–10.0 nM), and (b) corresponding linear fitting (R2 = 0.99745).
(a) Time-resolved fluorescence decay for BS-CDs in the presence and absence of DD, and (b) the development of a ground-state complex, BS-CDs·DD, from the interaction between BS-CDs and DD.

The cytotoxic effect of BS-CDs (0, 5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μL) with L929 cell by the standard MTT viability assay. L929 cells were incubated with BS-CDs for 24 h (images: a) untreated L929 cells, b) L929 cells + 5 μL BS-CDs, c) L929 cells + 25 μL BS-CDs, d) L929 cells + 50 μL BS-CDs, e) L929 cells + 75 μL BS-CDs, and f) L929 cells + 100 μL BS-CDs) (Scale bar 20 μm).
MTT assay was employed to determine the L929 cell viability (%) corresponding to different incubation concentrations of BS-CDs (0, 5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μL).
Fluorescence spectrum of BS-CDs interaction with S. aureus at different pH (3, 5, 7, and 8). a) control, and b) BS-CDs with S. aureus (λex 380 nm, λem 435 nm, slit width 5 nm).
Preparation of BS-CDs from bamboo stems, and their interaction with bacteria and detection of DD.
Detection of DD in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Result is obtained by an average of 2 or 3 fluorescence analyses that are repeated (mean ± S.D).

head.ce:title: Bamboo stem biomass waste-derived excitation-dependent carbon dots for nanomolar detection of fungicide dodine in real samples and their pH-sensitive bacterial interaction studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre for Research and Development, KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore 641407, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work investigates the mitigation of dodine (DD) fungicide residue, which is recognized for its adverse environmental consequences, by the use of carbon dots (CDs) prepared from bamboo stem biomass (BS-CDs). This work focuses on the synthesis of BS-CDs using the one-step hydrothermal method, which has been shown the remarkable sensing efficiency even at low concentrations. The synthesized BS-CDs were analyzed through X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic techniques (HRTEM). The BS-CDs were spherical and mono-dispersed, with an average size of 3.71 nm. By utilizing the fluorescence quenching property of BS-CDs, a detection limit of 4.3 nM for DD was successfully achieved. The non-toxic profile of BS-CDs has been confirmed by cytotoxicity studies using MTT assay. Application research mostly focuses on exploring the interaction between CDs and bacteria, hence elucidating their pH-sensitive characteristics. Real sample (fruits and vegetables) analysis was carried out to assess the viability of the detection approach, and the results showed respectable recovery rates (105 to 115 %) with a relative standard deviation (∼2 %). The findings provide a positive outlook for addressing the presence of DD residue in ecologically vulnerable regions, emphasizing the viability of using BS-CDs as an environmentally friendly remedy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass, a widely available carbon-rich resource, has a range of ecological benefits and is used extensively in several domains including wood waste management, biological processes, corrosion prevention, and wood-based building [1–6]. Bamboo, which belongs to the Poaceae family of grasses, has significant similarities to woody biomass, particularly in terms of its reduced ash percentage (1 %) compared to other herbaceous biomass materials (which often have ash content over 2 %) [7]. The prevalent accessibility and expeditious expansion of this material establish it as a widely embraced alternative to wood, mostly composed of cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose. Although bamboo has some beneficial characteristics, it poses certain obstacles in terms of the significant waste produced during the process of refining wood, namely from the stem portion [8]. The customary methods of disposal, which include the practice of dumping waste in fields or burning it, provide substantial risks to the environment [9]. Therefore, the implementation of an ecologically sustainable method for extracting lignin from bamboo stems is of utmost importance to not only tackle this environmental issue but also to maximize the value of excess bamboo waste materials.

Recent research has brought attention to the potential utilization of bamboo biomass waste to synthesize carbon-based nanomaterials [10–14] and be functional fillers in electromagnetic composites [15–19]. These studies have demonstrated the wide range of applications of such nanomaterials in various fields, including but not limited to metal sensing, electromagnetic interference shielding, absorption treatments, supercapacitors, and energy storage applications [20–26]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, for example, activated carbon (AC), and carbon dots (CDs), have shown remarkable adaptability in a wide range of applications, inhibiting microbes and bacteria, detecting tumors, spanning from electrical devices to biosensing and energy storage [27–31]. Zero-dimensional nanoparticles known as CDs have attracted considerable interest in the scientific community [32]. This is mostly owing to their distinctive properties, including the ability to adjust photoluminescence, the presence of surface functional groups, their compatibility with biological systems, and their minimal toxicity [33,34]. The aforementioned characteristics render compact discs (CDs) more favorable for utilization in many fields such as sensor technology, catalytic processes, analyte detection, and energy storage apparatuses [35]. Recent research has investigated the process of synthesizing CDs using bamboo leaves, intending to use them in fluorescence probes and antibacterial applications [36,37]. Nevertheless, the investigation of the use of CDs obtained from waste bamboo stem material to detect DD and its interaction with bacteria has not been extensively studied in scholarly existing works.

DD is a fungicide that is recognized for its little toxicity and is used as a preventative strategy against mold illnesses in a range of fruits, including apples, strawberries, pears, and peaches. The method of action of this substance entails its ability to readily adsorb onto the surfaces of microorganisms that possess a negative charge. This facilitates its entry into their membranes, leading to the eventual demise of the cells [38,39]. In addition to its agricultural uses, DD is used for the treatment, sterilization, and disinfection of industrial water, detergents, and seeds [40,41]. Nevertheless, the application of this substance has inherent dangers, such as the potential for skin irritation in its solid or highly concentrated liquid state [42]. The instability of DD in response to temperature fluctuations, its endothermic properties, and the effect on the fluorescence intensity of host materials highlight the need for a stable material that facilitates straightforward, expedient, and accurate analytical methods for the detection and quantification of DD [43]. In this context, the use of CDs in agricultural circumstances to detect DD offers a potential avenue for discerning the presence of this particular fungicide in soil or water. Furthermore, it is worth noting that bacteria with pH sensitivity in the same ecological context can react to the presence of DD or changes in pH levels. The examination of their interaction with DD or the investigation of the effects of pH fluctuations on their metabolic activities might provide further information about environmental circumstances.

Bamboo stem carbon dots (BS-CDs) were produced by a single-step hydrothermal process, employing bamboo stem biomass waste without the need for chemical activation. The BS-CDs had a spherical shape and had an average diameter of around 3.71 nm. The study showcased the emission characteristics of the subject under investigation, namely within the wavelength range of 310–380 nm, which were shown to be dependent on stimulation. The fluorescent BS-CDs were used in the detection of DD, to understand their sensing mechanism via fluorescence quenching. The innocuous properties of BS-CDs were confirmed by cytotoxicity experiments using the MTT assay. Additionally, an investigation was conducted to analyze the interaction between bioactive substances (BS-CDs) and bacteria, taking into account different pH settings. The experimental results demonstrated a significant increase in the intensity of the fluorescence emission spectrum at pH values of 7 and 8. In this work, the interaction of BS-CDs and DD with Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) has been extensively investigated. S. aureus is one the harmful bacteria that can affect the human respiratory system and can cause skin irritation. This combined approach allows the identification of particular interactions of BS-CDs and DD with S. aureus bacteria. These interactions might potentially have substantial ecological or agricultural ramifications. The use of BS-CDs in conjunction with DD detection and monitoring the interaction of BS-CDs and DD with S. aureus in an integrated manner provides significant contributions to the understanding of the environmental consequences and the efficacy of fungicide treatments in agricultural systems.

The raw material, bamboo stem, used as a precursor for this research work, was collected from the local area at Vallioor, Tirunelveli District, Tamil Nadu, India. The collected bamboo stem was dried at normal room temperature continuously for 30–40 days. After that, the bamboo stem pieces are eventually cut and crushed, then grind to obtain the powder.
All the Experimental solutions were made using deionized (DI) water from a water purification system (AQUOION TBD 200, Two Bed Demineraliser).

Spectrophotometers with the model number UV-1900 (SHIMADZU) were used to measure the UV–visible absorption spectral studies. Powder XRD patterns were made with a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer that used Cu-Kα radiation with a Ni filter (=1.5418 Å). At the Avinashilingam Institute for Home Science and Higher Education for Women in Coimbatore, India, an IR Affinity — 1S (MIRacle — 10) device was used to get FT-IR spectra. For Raman analysis, SAIF-MGU, Kottayam, India, used a Horiba LabRAM-HR with a 532 nm excitation laser source. At the SRM Institute of Science and Technology in Chennai, India, an XPS study was done with a ULVAC-PHI5000 Version probe III photoelectron spectrometer. For structural morphology investigation, FESEM (ZEISS (SIGMA), BRUDER) at Coimbatore Institute of Technology and HRTEM (200 kV FE_TEM, model JEM-2100F, JEOL) at SAIF, MGU, Kottayam were used. The lifetime of photoluminescence was recorded by an IBH time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system, and the decay curve was untangled by IBH data station software V2.6 at Bharathiyar University in Coimbatore, India. Cytotoxicity tests were done by the South India Research Association in Coimbatore. The photoluminescence emission measurements were carried out at the Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Coimbatore, using the fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse Fluorescent Spectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies). The Zeta potential analysis was performed on a Zetasizer Version 7.13 at DRDO, Life Science, Bharathiyar University, Coimbatore, India.

Hydrothermal treatment was involved in the synthesis of CDs using bamboo stem powder. 1.0 g of bamboo stem powder and 30 mL DI water were added into a 50 mL Teflon container. The Teflon with the mixture was packed into a stainless steel autoclave and kept in a muffle furnace for 24 h at 200 °C. After 24 h of treatment, the setup is cooled to room temperature. Then, the product was centrifuged to separate the suspension and hydrochar. Later, the suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μM membrane, and the suspension was used for characterization purposes.

The feasibility of BS-CDs for sensing DD was tested at different concentrations (from 0.1 to 10 nM) of DD. The stock solution of BS-CDs was prepared at a concentration of 10 mM. From the 10 mM stack solution, 1 mL of BS-CDs solution was taken and different concentrations of DD were added. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at an emission wavelength of 435 nm (λex = 380 nm) at 5 nm excitation and emission slit width. BS-CD fluorescence intensity was decreased by the addition of DD. The findings showed the sensor's outstanding DD specificity, consistency, and repeatability.

To examine the practical applicability of the current sensing method, the real sample analysis experiment was conducted on fruits (peach, plums, apple) and vegetables (onion leaf, kidney beans) purchased from nearby markets. The real samples were spiked with DD at 10 nM concentration, then BS-CD solutions were added to each real sample separately, and fluorescence measurements were recorded at λex 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial stain cultivation and BS-CD labeling
ce:section.ce:para: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, Gram-positive) cultures were prepared by picking the colonies from their corresponding master plates and inoculating them in 5 mL of Luria-Bertani broth separately. The broth was incubated in a shaker at 200 rpm overnight at 37 °C. After reaching an optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of 1.0, the culture was taken and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to pellet the bacterial cells. The supernatant was discarded, and the pellets were resuspended in MilliQ water. To investigate the influence of pH in bacterial interaction with BS-CDs, the pH of BS-CD was adjusted with 1 M NaOH to pH 3, 5, 7, and 8. For the experiment, 500 μL of the bacterial suspension was mixed with an equal volume of adjusted BS-CD. The mixture was incubated for 3 h at 37 °C and subsequently characterized using a fluorescence spectrometer.
The following diagram illustrates the one-step synthesis of BS-CDs from bamboo stem waste, their application towards bacteria interaction, and the detection of DD (Scheme 1 ).

X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out for BS-CDs to determine their crystallinity and structural properties. The (002) plane showed a large peak with 2θ values from 21° to 29°. Two additional wide shoulder peaks appeared within the 2θ range from 40° to 50° and 57° to 68°, attributable to the (100) and (103) planes (Fig. 1 ). These wide peaks justify the poor crystallinity of BS-CDs, which may be caused by the presence of several oxygen-containing functional groups and the proximity of the carbon atoms, which make BS-CDs amorphous. Due to the graphitic nature of graphene in BS-CDs, they occur in sp2 graphitic phases. Bragg's law was used to calculate the interlayer spacing of BS-CDs, which is almost similar to that of graphite (0.33 nm) [44]. In addition, the d-spacing of planes (002), (100), and (103) is calculated to be 0.28, 0.24, and 0.21 nm, which is similar to the graphite lattice spacing [45].

The FESEM analysis of BS-CDs reveals that spherical-shaped particles are densely packed throughout the entire surface. The particle size was found to be 15 nm (Fig. 2 ). The FESEM images also indicate that the particles are agglomerated due to the adhesion of particles by weak forces to reduce the surface free energy [46]. The presence of white dots was also found in the FESEM images of BS-CDs which are non‑carbonized entities from the bamboo stem. Small traces of silicon and potassium were also found in the elemental composition that is not annihilated under the temperature (200 °C) of the hydrothermal process [46]. The Energy-Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDAX) spectra and the percentage of elements in BS-CDs are given in Fig. S1. The maximum peaks were observed for carbon (54.76 %) and oxygen (43.88 %).

The HRTEM image shows that BS-CDs had uniform dispersion without obvious aggregation and spherical shape (Fig. 3a). The particles were counted to produce the size distribution graph (Fig. 3b), and Gaussian fitting was used to determine the average diameter, which was approximately 3.71 nm. A single particle image reveals a crystalline nature with a lattice fringe of 0.23 nm (Fig. 3c), which is equivalent to a graphite (100) plane [47]. The diffused rings in the Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern divulges the polycrystalline character of the synthesized BS-CDs (Fig. 3d) [48]. The lack of crystallinity of BS-CDs is attributed to the presence of more oxygen-containing functional groups which is further confirmed by the SAED pattern of the BS-CDs as shown in Fig. 3d [49]. According to previous reports, the carbon dots derived from biomass waste do not have uniform particle sizes.

The XPS confirmed the elemental composition and chemical bonding of BS-CDs. C1s, O1s, and N1s had prominent peaks at 284.7, 532.3, and 399.9 eV in the XPS survey scans (Fig. 4a) with surface atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen as 69.5 %, 25.5 %, and 5 %, respectively. Deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s spectra show peaks for CC at 284.77 and 286.07 eV, and CO at 287.89 eV, attributable to adventitious sp2 carbon bonds, which suggests three kinds of carbon bonds in BS-CDs (Fig. 4b) [50]. Fig. 4c shows the high-resolution O1s spectrum. The CO and COH/COC peak at 531.9 eV and 532.37 indicates that carbon‑oxygen bonds are present. Fig. 4d shows that the N1s signal at 399.91 eV implies that nitrogen is bound to carbon (CN) in BS-CDs [51]. In addition, the XPS chemical bond percentages were calculated for C1s (CC (54.61 % and 31.85 %), CO (13.54 %), O1s (OC (84.11 % and 15.89 %), and N1s (CN (100 %)). The XPS data reveals the presence of various oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of BS-CDs. These hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid functional groups in BS-CDs help them to dissolve in water [52].

Raman spectra of BS-CDs show a structural defect-related D band (disorder) at 1342 cm−1 (Fig. 5a) which suggests the amorphous carbon flaws in BS-CDs. The first-order scattering E2g vibration mode of the graphite sheet makes a G band (graphite) at 1568 cm−1 [53]. It also shows that BS-CDs have several carbon structures that look like graphite. The synthesized CDs are amorphous, not GQDs, as indicated by the 0.85 ID/IG ratio [54]. Intensity of the G band is brighter than the D band, indicating the presence of more sp2 carbons and hybrid carbons in it. The as-prepared BS-CDs are mostly graphitic carbon with sp3 defects [55]. The peak at 1442 cm−1 is attributed to the CH2 vibration in BS-CDs, which is due to the electronic states or scattered photon energy levels [56].

The FTIR spectra of BS-CDs show OH absorption bands at 3314 cm−1 and CH absorption bands at 2302 cm−1 (Fig. 5b) [57]. The peak at 2125 cm−1 is caused by the bending vibration of the aromatic CC bond [58]. The CC vibrations in non-aromatic molecules are shown by the peak at 1640 cm−1. The imide bands, non-aromatic double bonds, hydrogen bonds between CO groups of ketones and quinones, and stretching vibrations were observed at 1521 cm−1 [59]. Along with this, a peak at 1210 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching of C-OH, which can be designated as the existence of a hydroxyl group on the surface of BS-CDs [60]. The FTIR divulges that the BS-CDs had hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, but no other functional groups [61]. FTIR data supports the formation of BS-CDs with oxygen-containing functional groups which is in good agreement with XPS results.

The optical properties of BS-CDs were examined at room temperature. BS-CDs aqueous solution is yellow in natural sunlight but blue under UV light (365 nm). Fig. 6a shows that BS-CDs have two UV–Vis absorption peaks. The aromatic sp2 domain π → π* transition causes the modest absorption peak at 223 nm [62]. The CO or CN bond n → π* transition causes the highly intense and sharp absorption peak at 280 nm. This is due to capturing excited state energy from surface states [63,64].

The PL emission behavior of BS-CDs was studied at various excitation wavelengths (310–380 nm) (Fig. 6b). Interestingly, the PL emission intensity started to increase and showed a red-shift (435 → 450 nm) in the excitation wavelength from 310 to 380 nm. At specific excitation at 340 nm, the emission wavelength at 430 nm slightly changes due to the attachment of various surface functional groups to BS-CDs surface (indicated in Fig. 6b). Notably, at 380 nm excitation wavelength, the BS-CDs show the strongest fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm [65]. Noteworthy to mention that CDs are known for their excitation-dependent emission which has numerous obvious benefits in bioimaging. The calculated full widths half-maximum (FWHM) of the BS-CDs PL emissions is 57 nm. Thus, the BS-CDs with lower FWHM values have more pure colors which is beneficial for biological and sensing applications [66].

To examine the stability of BS-CDs, the zeta potential analysis was carried out. The stability information found through zeta potential might help to carry out biological studies [67]. The obtained less zeta potential for BS-CDs (−3.39 mV) in aqueous medium is due to carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups on their surface (Fig. S2). Moreover, the negative zeta potential of BS-CDs implies that the negative surface charges on BS-CDs have the potential to generate repulsion that prevents the particles from clustering. By the repellent columbic force, synthesized BS-CDs are generally stable without surface agents [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity studies of BS-CDs with dodine
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of BS-CDs towards DD was investigated against various fungicides such as Carbendazim, Mancozeb, Zineb, and Myclomain. These fungicides contain aromatic units similar to DD and are often used in fruits, such as apples [69]. Fig. 7 revealed that the fluorescence intensity of BS-CDs was increased by the addition of all the other fungicides except DD. This indicates that other fungicides could not quench the fluorescence intensity of BS-CDs and only DD could effectively quenched. The nature and degree of BS-CDs fluorescence enhancement were different for other fungicides [70]. These results demonstrate that the BS-CDs show a significant selectivity towards DD among the other fungicides under study. We noticed a gradual decrease in the BS-CDs fluorescence emission with the increasing concentration of DD which may support us in developing a ‘turn-off’ fluorescence sensor for DD via BS-CDs.

BS-CDs have a wide fluorescence emission peak at 435 nm (λex 380 nm). Fig. 8a shows that the fluorescence of BS-CDs was steadily decreased as DD (0.1–10 nM) was added sequentially to the BS-CDs solution. Two notable effects are responsible for BS-CDs behavior; (i) binding of DD with BS-CDs may affect the surface charge of BS-CDs, but successful coordination or chelation occurs between DD and BS-CDs, and (ii) availability of different -OH groups on the surface of BS-CDs might form a complex that quenches fluorescence of BS-CDs. The Stern-Volmer equation was employed to investigate the quenching behavior of BS-CDs upon the addition of DD (Fig. S3). The Stem-Volmer plot shows a satisfactory linear correlation (R2 = 0.99745) (Fig. 8b), suggesting a good static quenching mechanism of BS-CDs upon binding with DD. This current system estimated the BS-CDs detection threshold for DD as 4.3 nM.

To investigate the static fluorescence quenching of BS-CDs, we carried out a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) experiment in the presence and absence of DD (Fig. 9a). In the absence of DD, the triexponential function is the best fit for BS-CDs' fluorescence lifetime profile, which results in an average lifetime of 6.42 ns [71]. A low concentration of DD (0.25 nM) changed the decay curve from triexponential to biexponential with an average lifetime of 5.01 ns (Table S1). As the concentration of DD was increased (0.5, and 1 nM), the fluorescence lifetime (4.88, and 4.84 ns, respectively) and nature of the decay profile (triexponential to biexponential) of BS-CDs were altered, indicating the static quenching process. Since there is no significant change in the decay time of BS-CDs at the maximum concentration of DD (1 nM), the formation of a ground state complex, BS-CDs•DD, between DD and BS-CDs is confirmed through a static quenching mechanism (Fig. 9b) [72]. In other words, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor (BS-CDs) is decreased by increasing the concentration acceptor (DD) which implies that the fluorescence quenching of BS-CDs is dependent on DD concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DD in real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Given the possibility of interference from naturally occurring compounds, the investigation of the newly developed fluorescence probe (BS-CDs) is crucial for assessing its performance. In the currently proposed research, the standard addition method for the determination of DD in all fruit (peach, plums, apple) and vegetable (onion leaf, kidney beans) samples was followed. A standard solution containing 10 nM of DD was mixed into a 200 μL sample solution containing the spiked fruit and vegetable sample solution and 2.8 mL of BS-CDs. To determine the amount of DD in an unknown sample, fluorescent spectra were recorded before and after adding the standard solution to fruit and vegetable samples. The BS-CD fluorescence spectra (Fig. S4) of fruits and vegetables samples in the presence of standard solution were validated at excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 and 435 nm, respectively. The average of triplicate intensities was calculated as a standard deviation from the fluorescence spectrum. The detected recovery rates for DD were found to be between 105 and 115 %, respectively, with the RSD below 2 % (Table 1 ). These results indicate the excellent accuracy and recovery of the current method and its potential for detecting DD in real samples [73].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of the interaction of BS-CDs and DD with S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the bio-labeling properties of the probe, the BS-CDs and DD were used to interact with the bacteria S. aureus at different pH levels (3, 5, 7, and 8). The corresponding interactions were characterized using fluorescence spectroscopy. The efficiency of bacterial interaction between BS-CDs and DD with S. aureus was observed by fluorescence emission and is presented in Figs. 12 and Fig. S6, respectively. Under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the BS-CDs with S. aureus produce a blue emission at 435 nm. This indicates that the S. aureus was stained with BS-CDs. Notably, the control of BS-CDs shows a decrease in emission intensity as pH increases (3, 5, 7, 8) (Fig. 12a). On the other hand, the interaction of BS-CDs with S. aureus displays a fluorescence effect with low intensity compared to the control, particularly at pH levels 7 and 8 (Fig. 12b). A similar trend was also observed in the interaction of DD with S. aureus (Fig. S6). Moreover, a decrease in fluorescence intensity with the increase in pH was also evident from the fluorescence spectrum of both BS-CDs and BS-CDs with S. aureus bacteria. This observed trend suggests a correlation between the increasing pH and fluorescence intensity.

From FTIR analysis, it is confirmed that the carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups are present in the BS-CDs, which are anionic. The S. aureus contains cationic charged proteins, phospholipids, and lipopolysaccharides on its surface [74]. Hence, it might be possible that BS-CDs can bind with the cationic sites on the S. aureus cell wall due to electrostatic interaction which was established by the results of fluorescence studies in the particular pH (7 and 8). These outcomes may have a huge effect on pharmaceutical technology and environmental conservation.

We employed a one-step hydrothermal process to turn waste bamboo stem biomass into CDs. The amorphous nature of the as-prepared BS-CDs was confirmed through the observation of the D and G bands at 1342 and 1568 cm−1, respectively, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.85. The excitation wavelength-dependent blue emission of BS-CDs (λex/λem = 380/450 nm) was observed through UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The surface charge of BS-CDs was measured as −3.39 mV using zeta potential analysis. Microscope images confirmed that BS-CDs are spherical and have an average size of 3.71 nm. The spectroscopy results validate that the BS-CDs have oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface. The attained LoD (4.3 nM) through fluorescence sensitivity study is much less than the permissible threshold value of DD. The static fluorescence quenching of BS-CDs upon sensing DD was well supported by TCSPC results. In addition, the formation of a ground-sate complex, BS-CDs·DD, confirms the interaction of various oxygen-containing functional groups in BS-CDs with nitrogen-containing functional groups in DD. The selective bacterial interaction of BS-CDs with S. aureus was well recognized at physiological pH (7 and 8) through the fluorescence spectrum. These studies provide accurate and comprehensive information about the effect of DD and BS-CDs on human pathogenic bacteria. All these above-attained results suggest that the bamboo stem-derived CDs are an alternative material to detect toxic fungicides in ultra-low nanomolar concentration and are suitable for real-time agricultural and biomedical applications by satisfying the straightforward, environmentally benign, and sustainable approach.
Not applicable.

Subitha Adaikalapandi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. T. Daniel Thangadurai: Data curation, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. N. Manjubaashini: Formal analysis, Validation. D. Nataraj: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – review & editing. T.G. Satheesh Babu: Formal analysis, Validation. S. Megha Kumar: Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Author SA and TDT sincerely acknowledge the KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology for funding through an Institutional research fellowship, a Seed grant to conduct research, and for providing XRD and UV–Vis spectroscopy characterization facilities. NM admits her gratitude to the Department of Biotechnology, New Delhi for providing financial support under the DBT-Research Associateship program. DN would like to express his gratitude to the UGC-CPEPA program on Advanced Studies in Physics for the development of Solar Energy Materials and Devices of Bharathiar University for providing the facilities. Authors SA and TDT thank SAIF-MGU, Kottayam, for the Raman characterization studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The EDAX, Zeta potential, fluorescence lifetime, real sample analysis, interaction with bacteria, and LoD calculations of BS-CDs can be found in the supporting information of this manuscript. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110692.
